title: Rising seas could drown mangroves and release vast stores of carbon
summary:
While some previous studies have indicated that rising sea levels could boost carbon storage in mangrove ecosystems, a new study paints a more complicated picture.
"Research about carbon storage in mangroves is usually based on field observations, and such studies have found that carbon storage can increase as sea levels rise.
Greater Sea-Level Rise Means Greater Carbon LossThe team evaluated several sea-level rise scenarios developed by the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC).
The analysis showed that higher levels of sea-level rise produced increasingly negative effects on mangrove carbon storage.
Beyond storing carbon, mangroves play a critical role in shielding shorelines from storms, supporting wildlife, and helping sustain coastal communities.

title: Anger as Beddington incinerator stokes health fears
summary:
He described seeing large amounts of dust that he said was new to the area.
"Obviously dust is fine particles.
Most of it is in the air, but some of it will land on your windowsill," he said.

title: Trump admin’s cancellation of wind energy projects causes business turmoil
summary:
Seven northeastern states have sued US gov’t for paying TotalEnergies to withdraw from offshore wind projects.
On March 23, the administration reached its agreement with TotalEnergies to end the offshore wind leases for Attentive and Carolina Long Bay.
This is the first time developers have been paid to withdraw wind power leases.
In December, the interior department cancelled five offshore wind projects under construction in the US northeast.
The Trump administration has so far cut subsidies for solar projects, electric vehicles and other renewable energy, but offshore wind projects may be particularly hard-hit because it is a relatively developing technology.

title: Niger: Nearly 50 people die of thirst after lorry breaks down in Sahara desert
summary: 'It was either killed or be killed' - ongoing nightmares of an ex-child soldier in SomaliaYusuf Ali, now 34, struggles with his memories living in Mogadishu surrounded by dark reminders of his past.

title: A park famed for rare gorillas gears up to fight Ebola and protect its primates
summary:
A park famed for rare gorillas gears up to fight Ebola and protect its primatestoggle caption Roberto Schmidt/AFP/via Getty ImagesWhen Emmanuel de Merode looks around, it's a picture of serenity.
But beyond this picturesque scene, there is a volatile combination of brutal rebel violence and a burgeoning Ebola outbreak.
Sponsor MessageIn a region that's seen decades of bloody wars and a 2018-2020 Ebola outbreak, de Merode says the past few weeks stand out.
toggle caption Brent Stirton/Getty ImagesVirunga National Park is more than 180 miles from north to south.
The last Ebola epidemic lasted 18 months, and all the signs are that it'll be significantly worse this time.
